persons.arguments_against: Strongest counter-case: there are 5 Hootons in the staff lists and 2 Hooton biographies, so the Bermuda Attorney-General could in principle be the other Hooton or an unbiographed namesake; and the staff list shows no honours while the bio's subject held M.B.E. and Croix de Guerre. But the office is unique - a colony has exactly one Attorney-General - and the bio names it explicitly with the date: 'atty.-gen., Berm., 1958', immediately preceding the stint's 1959-1961 listings. The stint also ends precisely when the bio says he left for East Africa ('leg. sec. E.A.C.S.O., 1961'). For the counter-hypothesis, a second J. C. Hooton would have to hold the same unique post in the same colony in exactly the years the bio claims for this man. Absent honours in staff-list columns is common formatting omission, not contrary evidence. The counter-hypothesis fails.
persons.would_overturn: Evidence that a different J. C. Hooton (e.g. the second Hooton biography's subject) served as Bermuda's Attorney-General 1959-61, or a record placing this bio's subject elsewhere full-time during those years.

notes: Single bio in case, single stint. Unique senior office (Attorney-General of Bermuda), exact initials, appointment date 1958 in bio vs staff listings 1959-61, and departure date 1961 (to E.A.C.S.O.) matching the stint's last listed year. Multiple independent agreeing dimensions.
